10.1016/j.jmp.2019.04.003
As this volume and other recent offerings attest, the use of response times in the identification of mental architecture via systems factorial technology (SFT) has been a very fruitful endeavor (see, e.g.,  Little, Altieri, Fific, & Yang, 2017; Schweickert, Fisher, & Sung, 2012 ). We hope to add to this body of work with specific extensions of the basic framework to nested architectures, that is, serial or parallel channels occurring within serial or parallel channels. The present work is the first step in the process of elaborating basic SFT to nested architectures. Nested architectures may occur in many cases but one example derives from research investigating the nature and coordination of hemispheric processing in visual perception. Hellige, Kitterle, Christman, among others (e.g.  Hellige, Cowin, Eng, & Sergent, 1991; Kitterle, Christman, & Conesa, 1995 ) pioneered the use of presenting redundant stimulus patterns to both visual fields to disentangle the coordination of information across the hemispheres from the respective abilities or processing preferences within each hemisphere. We contextualize this paradigm with the example of detecting information at either the local or global scale of a complex pattern presented simultaneously in both visual fields. The hemispheres are known to exhibit bias with respect to information scale (for a review of basic findings, see  Ivry & Robertson, 1998 ) and this bias may be dissociable from interhemispheric coordination ( Hellige, 1995 ).

We begin with a brief overview of the basic concepts of architecture, descriptions of the empirical measures used for analysis within the SFT framework, and some assumptions and notation that will be used throughout the paper. We then elaborate on the redundant bilateral field paradigm and the specific ways we extend the basic architectures to derive results for predicted survivor and mean interaction contrasts for response times in this task. The list of specific architectures, together with stopping rules, and their predictions for interaction contrasts follow as proofs (or conjectures buttressed by simulation when analytic proof is not available). The complete results are then summarized in  Table 2 . Following the model propositions, we discuss how one might use these results to identify what the architecture is that may be at work in a typical paradigm in which bilateral trials are included and how these relate to constructs they represent. We conclude with a discussion of limitations and future plans for extensions to capacity analyses and empirical applications.

Originally, SFT was originally applied to the two — choice decision task in which a stimulus pattern carried information on one of two channels (or both) ( Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). As an example, consider the following stimulus pattern in  Fig. 1 . Suppose the observer is asked to respond ‘yes’ if either the local or the global structure contains an arrow shape ( Johnson, Blaha, Houpt, & Townsend, 2010 ). This task is called an ‘or’ task as an arrow on either the local or the global level produces a ‘yes’ response. When a signal is present in both channels (e.g., an arrow at both the local and global levels), it is referred to as a redundant target trial.

The scientific question addressed for this task is how might the information across stimulus levels be combined to determine a decision? Architectures originally studied for this basic task include three canonical structures: serial, parallel, and coactive. For illustrations of these, see  Little et al. (2017) , Figure 1.1 of that chapter. For an SFT analysis using a similar set of stimuli (rotated 90 degrees) to study individual differences and autism see  Johnson et al. (2010) .

If the system is capable of stopping as soon as the relevant information necessary for a decision is encountered, it is said to be self-terminating. If all channels are processed regardless of whether the relevant information necessary to determine a response is encountered earlier, we say that the system is exhaustive in its stopping rule.

SFT uses the dependent measure of response time (RT) across factorial conditions of salience of each information source to diagnose which of these three architectures is consistent with the data. The full paradigm also uses the data from single level (information only in one level but not both) conditions compared to the redundant information case to assess capacity limitations (see  Houpt & Townsend, 2012; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). We do not address capacity in this present work but focus exclusively on architecture structure. An extension into capacity predictions for nested architectures is planned for a future project.

We assume that the salience of the information at each level can be selectively influenced, for example, by adjusting the clarity of the shape information at the specific level. We embrace the definition of selective influence outlined in  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  in which an empirical factor is assumed to influence processing of only one subsystem. We assume that these manipulations impact processing time in such a way that better performance (increased speed) results from higher salience for the specific channel being influenced. A second notion concerns the level at which this influence is realized (e.g., at the level of mean RTs or cumulative distribution functions). We formalize this notion below.

RT from individual trials in this task is used to construct response time distributions for specific conditions ( Van Zandt, 2000; Van Zandt, 2002 ). For example, let

The basic architectures of serial, parallel, and coactive together with their assumed stopping rules and the assumption of factor selective influence predict specific patterns of behavior for these two statistics ( Little et al., 2017; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ).

This is a stronger condition than ordering of distribution functions in that single crossover density implies the distributions are ordered but not vice versa ( Townsend, 1990 ). Selectivity refers to the property that the system variable associated with the empirical factor is the only one directly impacted by the manipulation. Notions of nuance in the construct are actively being researched (e.g.,  Dzhafarov & Kujala, 2010 ). We simplify things in our developments with assumptions that preclude nonselective influence of the sorts investigated by Dhzafarov and colleagues ( Dzhafarov, Schweickert, & Sung, 2004; Schweickert et al., 2012; Zhang & Dzhafarov, 2015 ).

To facilitate understanding, we begin with a concrete example and a picture to help the reader understand our extension. We wish to model a specific type of trial that has been used in studies of interhemispheric interaction: the bilateral field trial (see  Fig. 2 ). The specific version we address here was introduced by  Hellige (1987)  to answer the question: if both hemispheres received the same information, which side would govern performance (an issue he termed metacontrol)? There are different versions of bilateral field presentations and tasks associated with them (see  Boles, 1995 ) that answer different questions. We focus on this specific one here as it lends itself to the redundant target situation that has been fruitful in many SFT applications.

To aid in the understanding of our extension, suppose an architecture shown in  Fig. 4 a is tasked to determine the response for a redundant target trial, the ‘or’ (is there an arrow in any stimulus?) case in the bilateral condition. Each visual field has its own processor, but these are organized in parallel across the two hemispheres. Within each field’s processor, the two stimulus levels are processed separately and independently by channels organized in parallel as well. Our experimental factors target the stimulus level within each pattern in both visual fields (either the local or the global level). If the stopping rules throughout are self-terminating, we can derive a representation of the total processing time of the system as a function of the processor and channel times and from that the SIC and MIC.

In order to generate baseline model predictions for all of the architectures considered, it is necessary at this stage to state explicitly the following assumptions that hold throughout the set of theorems and conjectures below:

• 
                               All random variables representing processing times are mutually statistically independent. For example,  
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           L 
                                           V 
                                           F 
                                        
                                     
                                   is independent of  
                                     
                                        
                                           T

                                           F 
                                        
                                     
                                  , etc. 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               Factors intended to influence processing of architecture subsystems target the local stimulus level (subsystem in each visual field) and the global stimulus level (also a subsystem in each visual field) as shown in  Fig. 4 . Specifically, if the local level is at a low salience, this is true for both fields simultaneously. Selective influence of the factors holds at least at the distribution ordering level and, when needed (as will be stated in the specific proofs) possibly at the single density crossover level. In models that have serially organized subsystems, the order in which each system occurs is not a function of the factor levels. Hence, if p  
                                     = 
                                   probability that the local level is processed first, it is unchanged by the salience of the local level (we also assumed  
                                     0 
                                     < 
                                     p 
                                     < 
                                     1 
                                  ). This may seem unrealistic until one considers that, in order for salience of part of a pattern to be perceived, that part must have begun to undergo processing. Because of this, we believe the most tenable effects of factors are on the rates of processing not the probability that the system will be chosen first. 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               We do not make any assumptions regarding the relationships (other than independence, see above) regarding processing time random variables of levels within fields and their counterparts in any other condition besides the bilateral trials modeled here. That is, in traditional paradigms assessing hemispheric processing characteristics, trials with stimuli in only one visual field typically occur. No proof below requires any assumption that these processing times may be related or identical to the corresponding processing times in the bilateral task. However, in a later section on Application, if one assumes that at least the architecture within a field is invariant from the single field to bilateral field presentations, one can use the single field trials to disambiguate model mimicry. We say more about that later. 
                            
                            
                               • 
                               We do not include a base time random variable as was done in  Townsend and Nozawa (1995) . We assume that our factors do not influence any component outside of the considered architectures and that any base time component is statistically independent of the processing time variables explicitly represented in the architectures. Under these assumptions, the base time can be safely excluded without loss of generality.

All random variables representing processing times are mutually statistically independent. For example,  
                                     
                                        
                                           T

                                  , etc.

Factors intended to influence processing of architecture subsystems target the local stimulus level (subsystem in each visual field) and the global stimulus level (also a subsystem in each visual field) as shown in  Fig. 4 . Specifically, if the local level is at a low salience, this is true for both fields simultaneously. Selective influence of the factors holds at least at the distribution ordering level and, when needed (as will be stated in the specific proofs) possibly at the single density crossover level. In models that have serially organized subsystems, the order in which each system occurs is not a function of the factor levels. Hence, if p  
                                     = 
                                   probability that the local level is processed first, it is unchanged by the salience of the local level (we also assumed  
                                     0 
                                     < 
                                     p 
                                     < 
                                     1 
                                  ). This may seem unrealistic until one considers that, in order for salience of part of a pattern to be perceived, that part must have begun to undergo processing. Because of this, we believe the most tenable effects of factors are on the rates of processing not the probability that the system will be chosen first.

We do not make any assumptions regarding the relationships (other than independence, see above) regarding processing time random variables of levels within fields and their counterparts in any other condition besides the bilateral trials modeled here. That is, in traditional paradigms assessing hemispheric processing characteristics, trials with stimuli in only one visual field typically occur. No proof below requires any assumption that these processing times may be related or identical to the corresponding processing times in the bilateral task. However, in a later section on Application, if one assumes that at least the architecture within a field is invariant from the single field to bilateral field presentations, one can use the single field trials to disambiguate model mimicry. We say more about that later.

We do not include a base time random variable as was done in  Townsend and Nozawa (1995) . We assume that our factors do not influence any component outside of the considered architectures and that any base time component is statistically independent of the processing time variables explicitly represented in the architectures. Under these assumptions, the base time can be safely excluded without loss of generality.

Table 1  has the guide to notation used throughout the following theorems and proofs. We now turn to the predictions for SIC and MIC for the nested architectures as applied to the bilateral trial task.

This section lists the full slate of architectures examined and their  
                            S 
                            I 
                            C 
                         ,  
                            M 
                            I 
                            C 
                          predictions offered as theorems with proofs or conjectures with simulation confirmation. To motivate the choice of architectures, we reconsider the bilateral trial condition and some thoughts regarding its use. For several decades, it has been known that, in the task of perception, the information processing approaches across the two hemispheres may differ qualitatively. Specifically, split-brain research ( Gazzaniga, 2000 ), neurological studies of effects of lateralized lesions ( Robertson & Lamb, 1991 ), response time studies using observers with normal intact brains ( Beeman, 1998; Kitterle, Hellige, & Christman, 1992; Kosslyn et al., 1989 ) all converge on the idea that the left hemisphere more efficiently processes local fine details (i.e., high spatial frequency content of patterns) whereas the right hemisphere excels with global, configural overall structure (carried in the low spatial frequency content of a pattern). One early question concerned if both visual fields were presented with the same information, and the task instructions could be answered based on information in one of the fields, would the hemisphere that excels at the task govern performance? This was the original motivation for the bilateral presentation condition introduced by  Hellige (1987) . 
                            1 
                          
                         
                            1 
                            There are other types of tasks that use bilateral stimulus presentation but have very different goals as clarified by  Boles (1995) . One example is to have stimuli appear bilaterally and ask the observer to indicate if the two are the same or different. Another type is to have different stimuli appear but information from one field is sufficient for a choice response. Architectures could be constructed consistent with these task instructions but would add additional complexity so, for this preliminary investigation, these are not considered here. 
                          Although several studies were conducted using the paradigm, the constructs of architecture versus workload capacity were conflated. Note that, under the conditions of simultaneous identical stimulus presentation across the visual fields, this task is essentially a redundant targets task ( Miller, 1982 ). For example,  Kitterle et al. (1995)  used violations of, and results consistent with, Boole’s inequality to argue about how information across the two hemispheres was integrated based on RT distributions from single field versus bilateral field trials for different kinds of spatial frequency content. We now know that this inequality is an issue of capacity ( Townsend, 1984; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ) not of architecture. Our plan in this study is to address both the architecture within hemispheres as it relates to global and local stimulus information as well as begin to incorporate theoretical ideas of across field architecture. We also note that the redundancy of the bilateral stimuli (that is, having the same stimulus in both fields) together with the nature of the response demands (monitoring both local and global channels in each field to detect information that varies orthogonally and independently across trials) seems to us to be the best experimental setup to promote the likelihood that the theorem assumptions will be met empirically (i.e., stimulus level and visual field independence together with selective influence). We believe that when new theoretical work is to be tested, fostering the initial ground conditions of the ‘null’ hypothesis models is the most desirable strategy. If theory fails here, extensions to cases in which assumptions will need to depart from the simpler independence/selective influence scenarios will not be justified.

The models studied are derived from combining the architectures of serial or parallel with the stoppage rules of self termination or exhaustive processing to achieve the full cartesian product of possibilities across visual fields or within fields. In each of the following model architectures, we state the theorem or conjecture, write out the initial model random variable representation, the

                          (when available)and offer a brief outline of the justification for the predictions. The  Appendix A  contains the full proofs for statements that afforded theoretical analysis. An online supplement provides model simulation details for support of the conjectures. Matlab code used for the simulations will also be available in the online supplemental materials.

Conjecture 
                               
                                  Under the stated assumptions, this architecture appears to produce an 
                                  
                                     S 
                                     I 
                                     C 
                                   
                                  that begins negative for smaller values of t and then becomes positive for larger values of 
                                  
                                     t 
                                  
                                  . In addition, simulations suggest that  
                                  
                                     M 
                                     I 
                                     C 
                                   
                                  may take on either a positive or negative value depending on the specific set of parameters (i.e., factor influences).

An online supplemental document provides details on model simulation and results.

Theoretical analysis did not yield a proof for  
                               S 
                               I 
                               C 
                             predictions, but did so for  
                               M 
                               I 
                               C 
                            . Both the conjecture and theorem are presented below.

                                     t

The two parts of this equation inside the brackets following  
                               p 
                             and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  1 
                                  − 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , respectively, are both negative by the assumption of selective influence. Hence, both  
                               S 
                               I 
                               C 
                             and  
                               M 
                               I 
                               C 
                             are always negative.

Theorem 
                               
                                  Under the assumptions outlined above, serial field, serial level processing with self-terminating stoppage on fields and exhaustive stoppage on levels yields an 
                                  
                                     S 
                                     I 
                                     C 
                                   
                                  that is negative for smaller values of  
                                  
                                     t 
                                   
                                  and then becomes positive for larger values of  
                                  
                                     t 
                                  
                                  ; it also yields an  
                                  
                                     M 
                                     I 
                                     C 
                                     = 
                                     0 
                                  
                                  .

This is a probability mixture of two serial exhaustive models.  Townsend and Nozawa (1995) , showed that  
                               S 
                               I 
                               C 
                             goes negative first and then positive, and the negative and positive areas are equal and thus cancel out rendering the  
                               M 
                               I 
                               C 
                               = 
                               0 
                            .

Theorem 
                               
                                  Under the assumptions outlined above, parallel field, parallel level on LVF and serial level on RVF processing with self-terminating stoppage on both fields and levels yields an 
                                  
                                     S 
                                     I 
                                     C 
                                   
                                  that is always positive; it also yields an  
                                  
                                     M 
                                     I 
                                     C 
                                     > 
                                     0 
                                  
                                  .

                               t 
                             increases,  
                               S 
                               I 
                               C 
                             goes from negative to positive, and that  
                               M 
                               I

Table 2  collects together all of the preceding  
                         S 
                         I 
                         C 
                       and  
                         M

                       predictions from Section  3 .

The full experimental paradigm would likely include single field trials as well as the bilateral trials.  Fig. 5  illustrates a typical empirical design that includes the different field conditions. Single field trials produce data that are analyzable within the original SFT framework described in many papers and books ( Johnson et al., 2010; Little et al., 2017; Schweickert et al., 2012; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). The present work adds predictions for the bilateral field presentation as it is specifically described (identical stimuli on both sides). The model mimicry in these bilateral predictions seen in  Table 2  will be disambiguated by the use of the single field data and the traditional results. This would, of course, require that the within field architectures are invariant across single field and bilateral trials. As an example, suppose that one observes an  
                         S 
                         I 
                         C 
                       that is strictly  
                         > 
                         0 
                       and an  
                         M 
                         I 
                         C 
                         > 
                         0 
                       on the bilateral trials. Four models are consistent with this pattern of results (Models 1–3 and 13). However, if an examination of the single field trials using SFT reveals that for the left visual field — right hemisphere (RH), the  
                         S 
                         I 
                         C 
                       is also positive (as well as the  
                         M 
                         I 
                         C 
                      ) but the right visual field trials — left hemisphere, (LH) produces an  
                         S 
                         I 
                         C 
                       that is flat and equal to 0, we can conclude that a parallel self-terminating model is operating in the LVF — RH whereas a serial self-terminating model describes the RVF — LH system. That is, model 13 is implicated overall. These conclusions do not depend on any parameterization of the processes and hold regardless of any capacity relations across one versus two field trials as they are based solely on the qualitative parameter-free SFT analysis.

Future work may examine a different type of empirical bilateral trial in which information is different across the visual fields. As the above analysis clearly illustrates, predictions from more complicated designs will be quite challenging to construct.

The present paper studied the behavior of the survivor interaction contrast and mean interaction contrast produced from specific nested architectures within the framework of systems factorial technology. Many SIC and MIC predictions in the form of theorems with analytic proofs were possible and those are general in the sense they hold for all classes of random variables embedded within the specific architecture. Other assertions did not lend themselves readily to analytic proof and suggestions for what to expect were made via simulations.

The phenomena that motivated this work were investigations of hemispheric specialization and coordination across hemispheres in the processing of hierarchically organized patterns (e.g.,  Hellige et al., 1991; Ivry & Robertson, 1998; Kitterle et al., 1995 ). Our laboratory is currently collecting data using the paradigm described in Section  5  together with recording electroencephalography (EEG) using the patterns of  Fig. 1 . These stimulus patterns were used to explore the information processing differences between observers on the autism spectrum compared to those who are not using SFT ( Johnson et al., 2010 ). In that study, patterns were only centrally presented (and were rotated 90 degrees to be pointing to the right) and the question of hemispheric laterality was not considered. Even so, the results described highlighted the importance of individual differences and fine-grained modeling in our understanding of information processing.

One of the great virtues of systems factorial methodology is the logical distinctions that are possible among the system concepts of architecture, capacity, and stopping rule ( Townsend, 1984; Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). As noted in the introduction, frequently these constructs have been conflated in published studies such that findings that apply specifically to capacity, for example, have been attributed to architecture ( Kitterle et al., 1995 ). Capacity refers to the processing efficiency of a subsystem or unit when the number of items to be processed by the whole system increases ( Townsend & Ashby, 1983 ). It has been formalized as a function of the integrated hazard function utilizing response times from single item trials and from redundant item trials (see  Townsend & Nozawa, 1995 ). Theoretically, the capacity of a system is dissociable from the architecture (the full cartesian product of capacity types and architecture types is logically possible) though these may be correlated in actual physical systems. The present work focused exclusively on architecture and stopping rules in considering our extension to the bilateral (redundant target) task. We intend in future work to continue the investigation of capacity with respect to the architectures we have studied here.

We remind the reader that the results described above correspond to a bilateral trial type in which the pattern is identical in both visual fields and that the factorial structure that is manipulated within the SFT framework is within pattern across the local–global scale. The results are theoretical and abstract and could, in principle apply to other nested architecture situations whose task instructions and structure produce similar possible scenarios. Future work may examine a different type of empirical bilateral trial in which information is different across the visual fields. That is likely to require modifications to the basic assumptions outlined above, such as accommodating the possibility of dependence. As the above analysis clearly illustrates, predictions from more complicated designs may be quite challenging to construct.

The present work focused on architectures that were limited to parallel and serial and their combinations. A third class of architectures, coactivation, included in the original formulation of SFT was not among the model architectures we examined in the bilateral task. It is an important means by which information is combined and needs to be studied. The challenge, which is evident from a review of papers examining coactive processes, is that many specific instantiations of coactivation are possible and each need to be separately explored. For example, in  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  the coactivation process presented was a Poisson channel summation model. Others are possible. Later research extended results to the Weiner process ( Houpt & Townsend, 2011 ). No doubt, to understand how coactivation operates within the bilateral paradigm will require the construction of specific instantiations. This will be a very important task as in some cases, ideas discussed in the empirical literature on hemispheric interaction evoke notions of coactivation that would be amenable to study using SFT methodology (e.g.  Doron, Bassett, & Gazzaniga, 2012 ).

We assume that the probabilities of stimulus level order do not depend on factors. Note that the product of survivor functions in each of the four terms in the above sum is equivalent to the survivor function of a parallel, independent minimum completion time model. Using Proposition 1 on page 328 of  Townsend and Nozawa (1995)  from these observations, we can conclude that

In this model, overall processing time depends on which level is processed first, then which level (local, global) is processed first within the field.

That is,  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        T 
                                        o 
                                        t 
                                        a 
                                        l

                                                       T 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       G 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       L 
                                                       V 
                                                       F 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 , 
                                                  if LVF first, and global level first given LVF 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       T 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       L 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       R

                                                  if RVF first, and local level first given RVF

Selective influence implies  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        H 
                                        i 
                                        g 
                                        h 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  > 
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        L 
                                        o 
                                        w 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     t 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  ∀ 
                                  t 
                                  > 
                                  0

Thus the two parts in the equation above inside brackets following  
                            p 
                          and  
                            
                               ( 
                               1 
                               − 
                               p 
                               ) 
                            
                         , respectively, are both negative. Hence we can conclude that  
                            M 
                            I 
                            C 
                          is always negative.

For this model,  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                     | 
                                     L 
                                     V 
                                     F 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           L 
                                        
                                        
                                           L 
                                           V 
                                           F 
                                        
                                     
                                     + 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           G 
                                        
                                        
                                           L 
                                           V 
                                           F 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                     | 
                                     R 
                                     V 
                                     F 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           L 
                                        
                                        
                                           R 
                                           V 
                                           F
